gallery.subtitle: AI-generated Eastern aesthetics artwork — ready to print on T-shirts, tote bags, and phone cases

artworkStories.tang_01: The dragon and peony are twin symbols of Tang Dynasty splendor. Dragons represent imperial power and cosmic energy, while peonies — called the 'king of flowers' — signified wealth and nobility. Together they adorned palace robes and temple walls, embodying the dynasty's golden age of art, trade, and cultural confidence along the Silk Road.
artworkStories.tang_02: The phoenix, or fenghuang, was the empress's emblem in Tang court culture. Paired with cloud scrolls borrowed from Central Asian textiles via the Silk Road, this design reflects the cosmopolitan spirit of Chang'an — then the world's largest city — where Persian, Indian, and Chinese aesthetics merged into something entirely new.
artworkStories.tang_03: Mandarin ducks have symbolized faithful love in Chinese culture for over two thousand years. In Tang poetry and silk embroidery, they appear beside lotus flowers — a Buddhist symbol of purity rising from muddy water. This pairing was a popular wedding gift motif, wishing couples enduring devotion and spiritual harmony.
artworkStories.tang_04: Peonies bloomed across Tang Dynasty art like nowhere else in history. Emperor Xuanzong reportedly planted thousands in the imperial gardens, and poets like Bai Juyi wrote odes to their beauty. The butterfly companion echoes Zhuangzi's famous dream — a philosopher who wondered whether he was a man dreaming of being a butterfly, or vice versa.
artworkStories.tang_05: The Nine Dragons Robe was reserved for the emperor alone. Each dragon represented a different cosmic force — sky, sea, earth, and the four cardinal directions. The ninth dragon was hidden inside the collar, visible only to the wearer — a reminder that imperial power ultimately answered to heaven, not men.
artworkStories.tang_06: Koi fish entered Chinese culture from ancient carp-breeding traditions, but the Tang Dynasty elevated them to art. Gold koi in churning waves symbolized perseverance and transformation — rooted in the legend of the Dragon Gate, where a carp that leaps the waterfall becomes a dragon. Scholars wore this motif hoping to pass imperial examinations.
artworkStories.tang_07: Celestial maidens, or feitian, drifted through Tang Buddhist murals at Dunhuang, scattering flowers and playing music in paradise. Unlike Western angels, they needed no wings — they flew on ribbons and clouds. Their flowing robes influenced Tang fashion, and aristocratic women styled their dress to echo these divine figures.
artworkStories.tang_08: The Eight Treasures — ruyi scepter, coin, lozenge, books, painting, jade chime, rhinoceros horn cup, and artemisia leaf — were a Tang Dynasty shorthand for abundance and good fortune. Scattered among peonies, they appeared on silk, porcelain, and lacquerware as blessings woven directly into everyday objects.
artworkStories.tang_09: Foo dogs, or shishi, guarded the gates of Tang palaces and temples. Brought to China via the Silk Road as gifts from Central Asian kingdoms, these lion-like creatures were reimagined through Chinese eyes — armored, fierce, and protective. Pairs flanked every important entrance, one holding a ball (yang), one holding a cub (yin).
artworkStories.tang_10: The plum blossom blooms in winter's coldest weeks, making it a symbol of resilience and hope. Paired with the oriole — a bird associated with spring's arrival — this motif appeared on Tang silk as a seasonal greeting, celebrating the turn from hardship to warmth, from darkness to light.
artworkStories.tang_11: The Eight Auspicious Symbols of Buddhism — wheel, conch, umbrella, canopy, lotus, vase, fish, and endless knot — arrived in China with Tang Dynasty monks returning from India. Arranged as a mandala, they map the path to enlightenment. This design was embroidered on temple banners and worn by devout aristocrats as spiritual armor.
artworkStories.tang_12: Tigers held a unique place in Tang cosmology as lords of the western sky and protectors against evil spirits. Unlike the dragon (emperor) or phoenix (empress), the tiger belonged to warriors and generals. Tang military banners bore tiger motifs, and soldiers tattooed tigers on their bodies to absorb the animal's ferocity.
artworkStories.tang_13: The Lantern Festival on the fifteenth day of the first lunar month was Tang Dynasty China's most spectacular public celebration. Emperor Xuanzong ordered fifty thousand lanterns hung across Chang'an, and citizens danced in the streets for three nights. Palace lanterns — elaborate constructions of silk, jade, and gold — became treasured art objects.
artworkStories.song_01: Song Dynasty painters elevated landscape to philosophy. A lone pine on a misty peak wasn't just scenery — it was a portrait of the ideal scholar, standing firm above the clouds of worldly ambition. Artists like Fan Kuan spent years in the mountains, believing that to paint nature truthfully, one must first become part of it.
artworkStories.song_02: The plum blossom was the Song Dynasty's most beloved subject. Poet-painter Lin Bu famously never married, saying the plum was his wife and cranes his children. Song literati saw in the plum's winter blooming a mirror of their own lives — cultivated, solitary, blooming against adversity, indifferent to whether anyone was watching.
artworkStories.song_03: Bamboo was the Song scholar's constant companion — in the garden, in painting, and in poetry. Su Shi wrote that he could live without meat but not without bamboo. Its hollow stem represented humility, its upright growth represented integrity, and its year-round greenness represented constancy. To paint bamboo well was to reveal one's own character.
artworkStories.song_04: Wild orchids growing from bare rock were a Song Dynasty metaphor for the uncorrupted scholar in a corrupt world. Unlike cultivated garden flowers, the wild orchid needed no human care — it bloomed in remote valleys for no audience but heaven. Su Shi and Zheng Sixiao both painted orchids after political exile, finding in the flower their own stubborn dignity.
artworkStories.song_05: Chrysanthemums bloomed in autumn when other flowers had faded, making them symbols of perseverance and late-life achievement. Tao Yuanming, the poet who inspired generations of Song literati, famously retired to grow chrysanthemums rather than serve a corrupt court. The flower became shorthand for principled withdrawal from public life.
artworkStories.song_06: Song Dynasty landscape painters invented a new way of seeing distance — not through linear perspective, but through atmospheric mist. Distant mountains dissolved into fog, suggesting infinite space beyond the frame. This technique, called 'level distance,' created paintings that felt like doorways into another world, inviting the viewer to walk into the mist.
artworkStories.song_07: The lotus pond at dusk was a recurring meditation in Song culture. Zhou Dunyi's essay 'On the Love of the Lotus' declared it the flower of the superior person — rooted in mud, rising clean, neither clinging nor repelling. Song painters returned to this image endlessly, finding in the lotus a model for how to live in an imperfect world.
artworkStories.song_08: Song bird-and-flower painting reached a technical perfection never surpassed. Emperor Huizong, himself a master painter, required court artists to observe living birds for years before painting them. A sparrow on a branch wasn't decoration — it was a complete world, every feather a meditation on the relationship between the particular and the universal.
artworkStories.song_09: Autumn reeds bending in wind were a Song Dynasty image of graceful acceptance. Unlike the rigid pine or upright bamboo, the reed bent without breaking — a different kind of strength. Poets wrote of hearing wind through reeds as the sound of time passing, and painters captured the melancholy beauty of things that yield rather than resist.
artworkStories.song_10: The moonlit pavilion was where Song scholars went to think, drink wine, and write poetry. Su Shi's famous 'Ode to the Red Cliff' was composed in a boat under the moon, and his 'Water Melody' — written missing his brother during the Mid-Autumn Festival — became the most beloved moon poem in Chinese literature.
artworkStories.song_11: Carp in still water was a Song Dynasty image of contentment and self-sufficiency. Zhuangzi's parable of the fish who didn't know they were happy in water resonated deeply with Song literati who had retreated from political life. The still pond became a metaphor for the mind at rest — clear, undisturbed, reflecting everything without grasping anything.
artworkStories.song_12: Winter plum was the ultimate Song Dynasty symbol — beauty that needed no warmth to bloom, fragrance that needed no audience to exist. The poet Lu You wrote over three hundred poems about plum blossoms, many after his forced separation from his first love. The winter plum became inseparable from the idea of love that endures despite everything.
artworkStories.guochao_01: Guochao — literally 'national tide' — emerged in the 2010s as young Chinese designers began reclaiming traditional symbols for streetwear. The dragon, once reserved for emperors, became a canvas for urban identity. This design fuses Tang Dynasty dragon iconography with contemporary street culture, asking what it means to carry ancient power in modern skin.
artworkStories.guochao_02: The phoenix has always been China's symbol of renewal — it dies and is reborn, it transforms rather than merely endures. In the Guochao movement, the cyber phoenix becomes a metaphor for Chinese culture itself: ancient roots, digital future, neither abandoning the past nor being trapped by it. Neon and tradition turn out to be natural partners.
artworkStories.guochao_03: Cloud patterns appear in Chinese art from the Bronze Age onward, representing heaven, transformation, and the boundary between worlds. Guochao designers discovered that these ancient forms translate perfectly into neon aesthetics — the same flowing lines, the same sense of energy in motion. Old symbols find new life when the context changes.
artworkStories.guochao_04: Chinese characters have been called the world's longest continuously used writing system — over three thousand years of unbroken evolution. Guochao streetwear treats hanzi as visual art, playing with scale, color, and context. A single character can carry centuries of meaning while functioning as pure graphic design. Language becomes identity becomes fashion.
artworkStories.guochao_05: The tiger in Chinese culture is the lord of all land animals, the embodiment of raw power and protective ferocity. In Guochao design, the tiger sheds its classical restraint and goes full street — bold outlines, saturated colors, the energy of a creature that has never been domesticated. Ancient guardian meets contemporary attitude.
artworkStories.guochao_06: The lotus has been China's symbol of purity for two thousand years. Guochao punk reimagines it as something that doesn't just rise above the mud — it transforms the mud itself. This design asks whether purity and rebellion are really opposites, or whether the most radical act is to remain beautiful in a world that keeps trying to dirty you.
artworkStories.guochao_07: The tension between ancient and future is Guochao's central creative energy. This design places Tang Dynasty motifs in a science-fiction context, asking what Chinese civilization looks like when it reaches the stars. The answer isn't a rejection of tradition — it's tradition as the foundation for something that has never existed before.
artworkStories.guochao_08: Red lanterns have lit Chinese celebrations for two thousand years — New Year, weddings, festivals, the return of travelers. In Guochao design, they become neon signs in a cyberpunk cityscape, suggesting that the future China imagines for itself is not a break from the past but its most vivid continuation. The lantern still means welcome home.
artworkStories.guochao_09: Koi graffiti is Guochao at its most playful — the ancient symbol of perseverance and transformation spray-painted onto the walls of the present. The koi that leaps the Dragon Gate and becomes a dragon is now leaping from tradition into contemporary culture. The transformation is the same; only the medium has changed.
artworkStories.guochao_10: The yin-yang symbol is perhaps China's most globally recognized image, but Guochao reclaims it from generic 'Eastern mysticism' and returns it to its philosophical roots. In a cyber context, the eternal balance of opposites becomes a meditation on the digital age: connection and isolation, speed and stillness, the human and the algorithmic.
artworkStories.guochao_11: The Forbidden City stood at the center of Chinese civilization for five hundred years, home to twenty-four emperors. Guochao designers treat it not as a museum piece but as living source material — its colors, proportions, and symbolic vocabulary remixed into something that belongs to everyone. The palace gates are open now.
artworkStories.guochao_12: Cloud Nine in Chinese tradition refers to the highest heaven, the realm of immortals and perfect happiness. In Guochao streetwear, it becomes an aspiration worn on the body — a declaration that the wearer belongs to something larger than the everyday. Ancient cosmology becomes contemporary confidence.
artworkStories.guochao_13: The dragon's pearl in Chinese mythology contains the essence of the universe, the source of all transformation. Guochao design reconnects this ancient symbol to its roots while acknowledging that for a generation raised on manga and games, the mythological and the pop-cultural are already inseparable. Ancient energy, new form.
artworkStories.ink_01: Splashed ink painting was invented by the Tang Dynasty eccentric Wang Mo, who reportedly painted drunk, splashing ink with his hair and hands. Song and later painters refined this apparent chaos into a discipline — learning to control the uncontrollable, to find landscape in accident. The tradition asks whether the artist makes the painting or merely releases it.
artworkStories.ink_02: The Zen brushstroke is the most reduced form of Chinese painting — a single mark that contains everything. Chan Buddhist monks practiced calligraphy and painting as meditation, believing that the quality of a brushstroke revealed the quality of the mind that made it. There is nowhere to hide in a single line. It is either alive or it is not.
artworkStories.ink_03: Abstract ink wash emerged from the literati tradition of valuing expression over representation. Su Shi argued that painting should capture the spirit of a thing, not its appearance. Centuries later, this idea produced works that look almost like Western abstract expressionism — but arrived there through an entirely different philosophical journey.
artworkStories.ink_04: Mist was the ink wash painter's greatest tool and greatest challenge. It could not be painted directly — only suggested by what was left unpainted. The white space between mountains was not emptiness but atmosphere, not absence but presence. Learning to paint mist meant learning that what you leave out is as important as what you put in.
artworkStories.ink_05: Bamboo shadow on paper was a classic ink wash subject — the shadow of bamboo cast by moonlight onto a white wall, then traced in ink. This practice blurred the line between observation and creation, between the world and its representation. The shadow was already a painting; the artist's task was simply to recognize it.
artworkStories.ink_06: Ink koi emerged from the tradition of painting fish in water — one of the most technically demanding subjects in Chinese art, requiring the painter to suggest both the fish and the water without painting either directly. The swirling composition echoes the Taoist concept of wu wei: effortless action, movement that arises from stillness.
artworkStories.ink_07: Rain on bamboo was a subject that tested the ink wash painter's control of time. The bamboo must look wet without looking heavy; the rain must be visible without being painted. Xia Chang, the great Ming Dynasty bamboo painter, said that to paint bamboo in rain, one must first feel the rain on one's own skin.
artworkStories.ink_08: The eagle in Chinese ink painting represents solitary power and clear vision — the ability to see the whole landscape from above while remaining perfectly still. Ink wash eagles are almost always alone, perched on a bare branch or cliff edge, looking at something beyond the frame. They are portraits of a certain kind of mind.
artworkStories.ink_09: The hidden dragon is a Confucian concept — the person of great ability who has not yet found their moment. In ink wash painting, the dragon appears and disappears in clouds and mist, never fully visible, always implied. This ambiguity is the point: true power doesn't need to announce itself. It is felt before it is seen.
artworkStories.ink_10: The cliff pine is the ink wash painter's ultimate subject — a tree that grows where nothing should grow, shaped by wind into something more beautiful than any cultivated garden tree. Song Dynasty painters climbed mountains to study these trees, finding in their twisted forms a model for how adversity creates character rather than destroying it.
artworkStories.ink_11: Ink portraiture in the Chinese tradition was less concerned with physical likeness than with capturing the subject's qi — their vital energy, their essential character. A great ink portrait might look nothing like its subject to a stranger, but the subject's friends would recognize it immediately. The face was a map of the inner life.
artworkStories.ink_12: The moonlit river was the ink wash painter's meditation on time. Water moves; the moon's reflection moves with it; the painter's brush moves across paper. Three kinds of movement, three kinds of time, captured in a single image that is itself already past by the time the ink dries. Su Shi wrote: 'The river flows on, but it is never gone.'

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: NemoClaw Culture ("we", "us", or "our") is committed to protecting your privacy. This policy explains what data we collect, how we use it, and your rights.
privacy.s1Body: We collect your email address when you sign up or join the waitlist. If you sign in with Google, we receive your name and profile picture. Payment transactions are handled by PayPal — we never store your card details. We also collect basic usage analytics (page views, feature usage) via Cloudflare Analytics.
privacy.s2Body: Your data is used to provide and improve our services, send transactional emails (order confirmations, waitlist updates), and respond to support requests. We do not sell your data to third parties.
privacy.s3Body: User data is stored in Supabase (hosted on AWS ap-northeast-1). Design files are stored securely and only accessible by you.
privacy.s4Body: We use Google OAuth for authentication, PayPal for payments, Resend for transactional email, and Cloudflare for hosting and analytics. Each service has its own privacy policy.
privacy.s5Body: You may request deletion of your account and data at any time by emailing hello@nemoclaw-web.com. We will process your request within 30 days.
privacy.s6Body: For privacy-related questions, contact us at hello@nemoclaw-web.com.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.intro: By using NemoClaw Culture, you agree to these Terms of Service. Please read them carefully.
terms.s1Body: NemoClaw Culture provides an AI-powered design platform for personal and commercial creative use. You must be 13 years or older to use this service.
terms.s2Body: You retain ownership of all designs you create. By uploading content, you confirm you have the rights to use it. You grant NemoClaw a limited license to display your designs within the platform.
terms.s3Body: AI-generated images are provided for your use. You are responsible for ensuring your use of AI-generated content complies with applicable laws in your jurisdiction.
terms.s4Body: Subscriptions are billed monthly or annually via PayPal. Early Bird pricing is locked for the lifetime of your subscription. You may cancel at any time from your dashboard.
terms.s5Body: We offer a full refund within 7 days of purchase if you are not satisfied. Contact hello@nemoclaw-web.com to request a refund.
terms.s6Body: You may not use NemoClaw to create content that is illegal, harmful, defamatory, or infringes on third-party intellectual property rights.
terms.s7Body: NemoClaw Culture is provided "as is". We are not liable for any indirect, incidental, or consequential damages arising from your use of the service.
terms.s8Body: For questions about these terms, contact hello@nemoclaw-web.com.

about.title: About NemoClaw Culture
about.heroHeadline:
Bridging Eastern Aesthetics
and Global Creators
about.heroSub: We believe traditional Eastern art belongs to the world — and AI is the bridge.
about.storyBody:
NemoClaw Culture was born from a simple question: why is it so hard for global creators to access and remix the rich visual heritage of Eastern art?

We started as a one-person studio, combining AI generation, professional design tools, and print-on-demand fulfillment into a single platform. Our mission is to make Eastern aesthetics — Tang dynasty patterns, Song dynasty minimalism, Guochao street culture, and ink wash traditions — accessible, remixable, and wearable for creators everywhere.
about.missionBody: Empower independent creators worldwide to discover, create, and share Eastern aesthetics — one design at a time.
about.value1Body: We celebrate Eastern art traditions with depth and authenticity, not as a trend.
about.value2Body: Every feature is built for creators — fast, intuitive, and powerful.
about.value3Body: Available in 4 languages, designed for creators everywhere.
about.teamBody: We're a small, passionate team. If you share our vision, we'd love to hear from you.

blog.title: Blog
blog.subtitle: Stories, tutorials, and updates from the NemoClaw Culture team.
blog.comingSoonBody: We're working on articles about Eastern aesthetics, AI art creation, and creator stories. Subscribe to the waitlist to be notified when we publish.

careers.title: Careers
careers.subtitle: Join us in building the future of Eastern aesthetics creation.
careers.introBody: NemoClaw Culture is currently a lean, AI-assisted one-person studio. We're not hiring full-time roles right now — but we're always open to collaborators, contributors, and creative partners.
careers.role1Body: Deep knowledge of Tang, Song, Ming dynasty art and cultural context. Help us ensure authenticity and depth in our content.
careers.role2Body: Independent artists and designers who want to feature their work on the platform and reach a global audience.
careers.role3Body: Help grow and nurture the NemoClaw creator community on Instagram, Pinterest, and beyond.
careers.howBody: Send us a note at hello@nemoclaw-web.com with the subject line "Collaboration" and tell us about yourself.
